text.content: 3.1  Real World Bridge  [s3_1_real_world_bridge]

rich_text.plain_text: 3.1  Real World Bridge  [s3_1_real_world_bridge]

text.content: "Graphs aren't just in math class. People use them everywhere. Look at these three scenarios. Which of these could use a bar graph to show the data? Select all that apply."

rich_text.plain_text: "Graphs aren't just in math class. People use them everywhere. Look at these three scenarios. Which of these could use a bar graph to show the data? Select all that apply."

text.content:
tool: multi_select  options: ["Your class votes on a field trip destination", "Library tracks which book genre students check out most", "Cafeteria tracks which lunch is most popular"]
"Which of these could use a bar graph to show the data? Select all that apply."

rich_text.plain_text:
tool: multi_select  options: ["Your class votes on a field trip destination", "Library tracks which book genre students check out most", "Cafeteria tracks which lunch is most popular"]
"Which of these could use a bar graph to show the data? Select all that apply."

text.content: ✅ Student selected all three scenarios, correct

rich_text.plain_text: ✅ Student selected all three scenarios, correct

text.content: "Exactly. All of them! Any time you're counting and comparing categories, a graph can help."

rich_text.plain_text: "Exactly. All of them! Any time you're counting and comparing categories, a graph can help."

text.content: ❌ Branch 2: Student selected only correct answers but not all three

rich_text.plain_text: ❌ Branch 2: Student selected only correct answers but not all three

text.content: "You're on the right track. Each one you picked could use a bar graph. Look at the scenarios again. Are there any others that count and compare categories?"

rich_text.plain_text: "You're on the right track. Each one you picked could use a bar graph. Look at the scenarios again. Are there any others that count and compare categories?"

text.content: ❌ Branch 3: Student selected no correct answers

rich_text.plain_text: ❌ Branch 3: Student selected no correct answers

text.content: "Let's think about what bar graphs show. They count things in different categories so you can compare them. Which scenarios count and compare?"

rich_text.plain_text: "Let's think about what bar graphs show. They count things in different categories so you can compare them. Which scenarios count and compare?"

text.content: ❌ Branch 4: Student selected mix of correct and incorrect answers

rich_text.plain_text: ❌ Branch 4: Student selected mix of correct and incorrect answers

text.content: "Some of your picks are right. Those scenarios do count and compare categories. Check your other selections. Do they count things in categories?"

rich_text.plain_text: "Some of your picks are right. Those scenarios do count and compare categories. Check your other selections. Do they count things in categories?"

text.content: ❌ Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

rich_text.plain_text: ❌ Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

text.content: "Let me show you how this works. Bar graphs count things in different categories. Field trips: you count votes for each destination. Library books: you count checkouts for each genre. Cafeteria: you count how many students chose each lunch. All three count and compare, so all three could use a bar graph. The answer is all of them."

rich_text.plain_text: "Let me show you how this works. Bar graphs count things in different categories. Field trips: you count votes for each destination. Library books: you count checkouts for each genre. Cafeteria: you count how many students chose each lunch. All three count and compare, so all three could use a bar graph. The answer is all of them."

text.content: "Graphs help people see patterns and make decisions. You'll see them in school, in stores, on the news, everywhere."

rich_text.plain_text: "Graphs help people see patterns and make decisions. You'll see them in school, in stores, on the news, everywhere."

text.content: 3.2 Metacognitive Comparison Questions [s3_2_metacognitive_comparison_questions]

rich_text.plain_text: 3.2 Metacognitive Comparison Questions [s3_2_metacognitive_comparison_questions]

text.content: "You saw two kinds of comparison questions today. Some asked "how many more" and others asked "how many fewer." To answer comparisonquestions, what do you need to do?"

rich_text.plain_text: "You saw two kinds of comparison questions today. Some asked "how many more" and others asked "how many fewer." To answer comparisonquestions, what do you need to do?"

text.content:
tool: multiple_choice  options: Add the categories, Subtract the categories, Find each value
"To answer comparison questions like "how many more" and "how many fewer", what do you need to do?"

rich_text.plain_text:
tool: multiple_choice  options: Add the categories, Subtract the categories, Find each value
"To answer comparison questions like "how many more" and "how many fewer", what do you need to do?"

text.content: ✅ Student answered subtract — correct, both 'how many more' and 'how many fewer' require subtraction  [if selected = Subtract the categories]

rich_text.plain_text: ✅ Student answered subtract — correct, both 'how many more' and 'how many fewer' require subtraction  [if selected = Subtract the categories]

text.content: "How many more" and "how many fewer"

rich_text.plain_text: "How many more" and "how many fewer"

text.content: both require subtraction. That's called COMPARING. You find the difference to compare. And remember: "in all" means COMBINE, so you add. {Compare} and {combine}, those are your two problem types."

rich_text.plain_text: both require subtraction. That's called COMPARING. You find the difference to compare. And remember: "in all" means COMBINE, so you add. {Compare} and {combine}, those are your two problem types."

text.content: ❌ Student selected 'Add the categories' — misconception that comparison requires addition  [if selected = Add the categories]

rich_text.plain_text: ❌ Student selected 'Add the categories' — misconception that comparison requires addition  [if selected = Add the categories]

text.content: "Adding gives you a total, not a comparison. When we compare with "more" or "fewer" we're finding the difference between two amounts. What operation do we need?"

rich_text.plain_text: "Adding gives you a total, not a comparison. When we compare with "more" or "fewer" we're finding the difference between two amounts. What operation do we need?"

text.content: ❌ Student selected 'Find each value' — partial understanding, but missing the key operation  [if selected = Find each value]

rich_text.plain_text: ❌ Student selected 'Find each value' — partial understanding, but missing the key operation  [if selected = Find each value]

text.content: "Yes, you need each value first. But then what? Both "how many more" and "how many fewer" ask you to compare by finding the difference. What operation is that?"

rich_text.plain_text: "Yes, you need each value first. But then what? Both "how many more" and "how many fewer" ask you to compare by finding the difference. What operation is that?"

text.content: ❌ Student answered incorrectly — system models the correct answer  [fallback]

rich_text.plain_text: ❌ Student answered incorrectly — system models the correct answer  [fallback]

text.content: "Let's think through this together. When you see "how many more" or "how many fewer," both questions ask you to compare. To compare, you subtract the smaller amount from the larger amount. That's how you find the difference. Comparison questions require subtraction."

rich_text.plain_text: "Let's think through this together. When you see "how many more" or "how many fewer," both questions ask you to compare. To compare, you subtract the smaller amount from the larger amount. That's how you find the difference. Comparison questions require subtraction."

text.content: "Also remember: "in all" means COMBINE, so you add. {Compare} and {combine}, those are your two problem types."

rich_text.plain_text: "Also remember: "in all" means COMBINE, so you add. {Compare} and {combine}, those are your two problem types."
